content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 20,
1994
Duane Wallace
Member
South Tahoe Public Utility District
P.O.
Box 7743
South Lake Tahoe,
CA
96158
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—94-184
Dear Mr.
Wallace:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
Since your
advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision,
we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTIONS
1.
You are an elected board member of a special district
called the South Tahoe Public Utility District.
If you are
employed by the local Chamber of Commerce as Executive Director,
under what circumstances would the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Act require you to disqualify yourself from participating
in district decisions, particularly decisions concerning the City
of South Lake Tahoe which funds the Chamber of Commerce?
2.
Would the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act
prohibit you from using your official position with the district
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000
— 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807
•
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-94-184
Page
2
to influence the decisions of the City of South Lake Tahoe
concerning the Chamber of Commerce?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
If you are employed by the local Chamber of Commerce as
Executive Director, the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act
would require your disqualification from district decisions,
including decisions concerning the City of South Lake Tahoe, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the Chamber of Commerce will be
materially financially affected by the district decisions.
If
disqualification is required,
you would not be able to make,
participate in making,
or use your official position to influence
the district decisions.
If the Chamber of Commerce appeared directly before the
district as an applicant or the subject of a decision,
or there
exists a nexus in the governmental decision in question, the
Chamber of Commerce would be deemed to be materially affected by
the district decisions.
Moreover, if a district decision had an indirect financial
effect on the Chamber of Commerce,
you would be required to
disqualify yourself from a district decision if the Chamber of
Commerce,
a nonprofit entity,
would be materially affected as set
forth in Regulation 18702.5.
2.
As an elected official of the special district,
you
would be required to disqualify yourself from using your official
position with the district to influence any decisions of another
agency,
such as the City of South Lake Tahoe,
which would have a
reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
Chamber of Commerce.
FACTS
You are an elected board member of a special district, the
South Tahoe Public Utility District
(the “district”).
You are considering applying for a job with the local Chamber
of Commerce as Executive Director.
The Chamber of Commerce is a
private nonprofit corporation which receives, funds for the City of
South Lake Tahoe.
The city collects the funds provided to the
Chamber of Commerce from a Transient Occupancy Tax.
Occasionally, the City of South Lake Tahoe and the district
have business to conduct due to their similar geographic
jurisdictions.


Our File No.
1—94—184
Page
3
ANALYSIS
Conflicts of Interests, Generally
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
As a member of the South Tahoe
Public Utility District,
you are a “public official” as defined in
the Act.
(Section 82048.)
Financial Interest
A public official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(c).
Since,
presumably, the Chamber of Commerce would be a source
of income to you for purposes of Section 87103(c) if you were
hired as Executive Director, the Chamber of Commerce would be a
potentially disqualifying economic interest of yours.
Therefore,
you could not make, participate in making,
or attempt to use your
official position to influence a governmental decision if the
decision would have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally,
on you personally or your source of income, the Chamber
of Commerce.
Making, Participate in Making or Using Official Position to
Influence
For purposes of Section 87100,
a public official:
1.
Makes a governmental decision whenever the public
official acting within the authority of his or her office or
position votes on a matter,
commits his or her agency to a course


Our File No.
1—94—184
Page
4
of action,
or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of
the agency.
(Regulation 18700(b).)
2.
Participates in a governmental decision when, acting
within the authority of his or her position, the public official
negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a
governmental entity or private person regarding the decision or
advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker, either
directly or without significant intervening substantive review.
(Regulation 18700(c).)
3.
Attempts to influence a decision where the official:
(a)
Contacts or appears before any member, officer,
employee
of the official’s agency or any agency under the appointive or
budgetary control of the official’s agency.
(Regulation
18700.1(a).)
(b)
Attempts to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision of an agency,
3 other than the
official’s own agency,
where the official acts or purports to act
on behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her own agency
to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the agency
making the decision.
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)
Based on the above,
for example, if you had a conflict of
interest by virtue of the income you would receive from the
Chamber of Commerce, not only would you be prohibited from voting,
but you could not contact any official of your agency to discuss
the matter for any purpose whatsoever.
Moreover,
you would be precluded from using your official
position with the district to influence the decisions of another
agency,
such as the City of South Lake Tahoe, if,
for the purpose
of influencing the city’s decision,
you acted or purported to act
on behalf of,
or as the representative of, the district.
Such
actions include,
but are not limited to the use of official
stationery.
(Regulation 18700.1(c).)
However, with respect to
other agencies such as the City of South Lake Tahoe,
you would not
be precluded from influencing the city in your private capacity.
Materiality
As noted above, the effect of a decision must be reasonably
“Agency” means any state agency or local government agency.
(Section 82003.)


Our File No.
1-94-184
Page
5
4
foreseeable
and material on the official’s economic interest for
disqualification to be required.
Regulation 18702 et seq. sets
forth the guidelines for determining whether an official’s
economic interest in a decision is “materially” affected as
required by Section 87103.
If an official’s source of income is
directly involved in the decision as an applicant or the sub-i ect
a decision, the effect of the decision on the source of income
is deemed material and disqualification is required.
(Regulation
18702.1(a).)
A source of income would be directly involved in a decision
before an official’s agency when that person or entity, either
personally or by an agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application,
claim,
appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in,
or is the subject
of, the proceeding concerning the decision before
the official or the official’s agency.
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of
a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance,
renewal,
approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit,
or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
(Regulation 18702.1(b).)
Thus, the Chamber of Commerce would be an applicant or the
subject of a decision before the district when the Chamber of
Commerce initiates a proceeding by filing an application,
claim
appeal,
or similar request,
or is a named party in,
or the
subject,
of the proceeding before the district.
(Regulation
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur.
To be foresee
able,
the effects of a decision must be more than a mere
possibility; however certainty is not required.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community Development Com.
(1987)
196 Cal.
App.
3d 983,
989—991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.
App.
3d 817,
822; In re
Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest; it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest.
(Witt v.
Morrow,
supra at- 823.)


Our File No.
1-94-184
Page
6
18702.1(b); Richards Advice Letter,
No.
I—92—024.)
The effect of a decision is also deemed material on a source
of income if there exists a nexus in the governmental decision in
question.
A “nexus” would exist if you receive income to achieve
a goal or purpose which would be achieved,
defeated,
aided,
or
hindered by the governmental decision.
(Regulation 18702.1,
subdivisions
(a) (1)
and (d).)
The rationale underlying the “nexus” test is that a public
official may not accomplish in his or her public capacity what the
public official is paid to accomplish in his or her private
capacity.
(Best Advice Letter,
No. A-8l-032.)
Moreover,
when a
connection exists between a person’s job and his or her role as a
public official, there is a presumption that the value of the
employee’s services to the employer is based at least in part on
the fact that the employee is a public official.
For example,
in
the Best Advice Letter,
supra,
we advised:
“if
[the nonprofit]
took a position on a particular proposal before
[the agency], your
disqualification on the matter would be required.”
Nexus can exist in other contexts as well.
For example,
in
the Cornelius Advice Letter,
No.
A-82-104
we advised:
“If you
vote on a decision to provide a grant to the [nonprofit],
you are
accomplishing as a public official what you are paid to do in your
position with the [nonprofit].”
(Richards Advice Letter,
supra.)
In the Richards Advice Letter,
supra,
we advised that if a city
councilmember received income from a Chamber of
Commerce to
produce an RV/Outdoor promotion,
a nexus may exist between the
councilmember’s work for the Chamber of Commerce and the
official’s position as city councilinember.
Whether “nexus” applies would depend on the nature of the
decision.
However,
where applicable,
you would be prohibited from
making, participating in making,
or using your official position
to influence district decisions,
or using your official position
to influence decisions of the City of South Lake Tahoe.
You stated in our telephone conversation of May 23,
1994,
that you are concerned about district decisions concerning the
In addition,
Regulation 18702.1(a) (4)
provides the effect of a
decision is material if the decision will result in the personal
expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities of the official or his or
her immediate family increasing by at least $250.
Therefore,
you
would be required to disqualify yourself from making,
participating in making,
or using your official position to
influence any district decision,
for example, specifically
concerning the funding by the district of your position as
Executive Director with the Chamber of Commerce.


Our File No.
1-94-184
Page
7
City of South Lake Tahoe because the city funds the Chamber of
Commerce.
Under such circumstances,
you would not be precluded
from participating in district decisions affecting the City of
South Lake Tahoe merely because the Chamber of Commerce is funded
by the City of South Lake Tahoe,
so long as the Chamber of
Commerce would not be financially affected by decisions of your
agency as discussed above.
2.
Indirect Effects
However,
even if the Chamber of Commerce is not directly
involved in a decision of the district,
you would still be
required to disqualify yourself from making, participating in
making or influencing decisions of your agency,
or from
influencing the decisions of another agency in your official
capacity, if the Chamber of Commerce would be indirectly
materially affected by the decision.
We do not have any facts
concerning governmental decisions which may indirectly affect the
Chamber of Commerce.
Thus,
we can only provide you this general
guidance.
However, to determine whether an indirect effect on a
source of income which is a nonprofit entity is material you would
apply Regulation 18702.5
(copy enclosed).
For a relatively small nonprofit entity, Regulation
18702.5(f) provides that effect of a decision is material as to a
nonprofit entity which is a source of income to an official if the
following applies:
(f)
For an entity whose gross annual receipts
are $100,000 or less, the effect of the decision
will be any of the following:
(1)
The decision will result in an
increase or decrease of the entity’s gross
annual receipts for a fiscal year in the
amount of $10,000 or more.
(2)
The decision will cause the entity
to incur or avOid additional expenses or to
reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more.
(3)
The decision will result in an
increase or decrease in the value of the
entity’s assets or liabilities in the amount
of $10,000 or more.
You should consult the regulation to determine the applicable
standard.


Our File No.
1—94—184
Page
8
Public Generally
Finally, if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703
(copy enclosed.)
You have
provided no facts to determine whether the “public generally”
exception would apply.
However, it is unlikely that it would
apply to funding decisions concerning the Chamber of Commerce, for
example.
However, if you believe the exception would apply,
you
should contact us for further advice.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.
Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
(2)72C
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
Enclosure

a nonprofit entity,
would be materially affected as set

Finally, if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703
(copy enclosed.)
You have
provided no facts to determine whether the “public generally”
exception would apply.
However, it is unlikely that it would
apply to funding decisions concerning the Chamber of Commerce, for
example.
However, if you believe the exception would apply,
you
should contact us for further advice.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.
Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.

called the South Tahoe Public Utility District.

under what circumstances would the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Act require you to disqualify yourself from participating

of South Lake Tahoe which funds the Chamber of Commerce?

prohibit you from using your official position with the district

to influence the decisions of the City of South Lake Tahoe

CONCLUSION: 1.
If you are employed by the local Chamber of Commerce as
Executive Director, the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act
would require your disqualification from district decisions,
including decisions concerning the City of South Lake Tahoe, if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the Chamber of Commerce will be
materially financially affected by the district decisions.
If
disqualification is required,
you would not be able to make,
participate in making,
or use your official position to influence
the district decisions.
If the Chamber of Commerce appeared directly before the
district as an applicant or the subject of a decision,
or there
exists a nexus in the governmental decision in question, the
Chamber of Commerce would be deemed to be materially affected by
the district decisions.

effect on the Chamber of Commerce,
you would be required to
disqualify yourself from a district decision if the Chamber of
Commerce,
a nonprofit entity,
would be materially affected as set
forth in Regulation 18702.5.
2.
As an elected official of the special district,
you

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission June 20, 1994 Duane Wallace Member South Tahoe Public Utility District P.O. Box 7743 South Lake Tahoe, CA 96158 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1—94-184 Dear Mr. Wallace: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”). 1 Since your advice request does not refer to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 QUESTIONS 1. You are an elected board member of a special district called the South Tahoe Public Utility District. If you are employed by the local Chamber of Commerce as Executive Director, under what circumstances would the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act require you to disqualify yourself from participating in district decisions, particularly decisions concerning the City of South Lake Tahoe which funds the Chamber of Commerce? 2. Would the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act prohibit you from using your official position with the district 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000 — 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3).) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. 1-94-184 Page 2 to influence the decisions of the City of South Lake Tahoe concerning the Chamber of Commerce? CONCLUSIONS 1. If you are employed by the local Chamber of Commerce as Executive Director, the conflict—of—interest provisions of the Act would require your disqualification from district decisions, including decisions concerning the City of South Lake Tahoe, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the Chamber of Commerce will be materially financially affected by the district decisions. If disqualification is required, you would not be able to make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence the district decisions. If the Chamber of Commerce appeared directly before the district as an applicant or the subject of a decision, or there exists a nexus in the governmental decision in question, the Chamber of Commerce would be deemed to be materially affected by the district decisions. Moreover, if a district decision had an indirect financial effect on the Chamber of Commerce, you would be required to disqualify yourself from a district decision if the Chamber of Commerce, a nonprofit entity, would be materially affected as set forth in Regulation 18702.5. 2. As an elected official of the special district, you would be required to disqualify yourself from using your official position with the district to influence any decisions of another agency, such as the City of South Lake Tahoe, which would have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the Chamber of Commerce. FACTS
